title: Tetanus
authors: C. Louise Thwaites, Hai Duong Ha Thi

items.title: Understanding Tetanus Diagnosis and Case Definitions
items.content: Tetanus diagnosis relies heavily on clinical manifestations due to limited laboratory confirmation. The CDC defines probable tetanus based on acute illness with muscle spasms or hypertonia, while the WHO provides specific definitions for neonatal and maternal tetanus.
keyPoints: Diagnosis is primarily clinical with limited lab support.
CDC's probable tetanus definition: acute illness with muscle spasms/hypertonia in absence of a more likely diagnosis.
WHO defines neonatal tetanus as loss of sucking/crying ability between days 3-28, with rigidity and spasms.
Maternal tetanus is defined by WHO as occurring during pregnancy or within 6 weeks post-pregnancy.

items.title: Etiology of Tetanus: Entry Points and Risk Factors
items.content: C. tetani spores enter the body through abrasions, wounds, or umbilical stumps in neonates, thriving in anaerobic environments to release neurotoxin. Lack of vaccination increases risk.
keyPoints: Tetanus is caused by C. tetani, a gram-positive, spore-forming bacterium.
Spores enter through abrasions, wounds, or umbilical stumps; 20-30% cases have unclear entry points.
Superficial limb abrasions are common in adults; deeper injuries lead to severe presentations.
Neonatal tetanus often results from unhygienic umbilical care, circumcision, or ear-piercing.
Risk increases with lack of vaccination or incomplete immunization history.

items.title: Tetanus Epidemiology: Impact of Vaccination and Risk Factors
items.content: The incidence of tetanus has decreased significantly in developed countries due to increased vaccination coverage. In the U.S., most cases occur in unvaccinated individuals, with a notable risk among those aged 20-64 years. Diabetes and intravenous drug use are associated with higher risk.
keyPoints: Tetanus incidence is low in developed countries due to vaccines.
28 tetanus cases reported in the U.S. in 2022.
Most cases occur in unvaccinated individuals; booster shots recommended every 10 years.
64% of U.S. cases (2009-2017) occurred in ages 20-64, with only 13% under age 20.
Diabetes and intravenous drug use increase tetanus risk.

items.title: Tetanus Pathogenesis: Mechanism of Toxin Action in the CNS
items.content: Tetanus toxin, produced by C. tetani, is transported retrogradely into the CNS, binding to motor neuron terminals and inhibiting neurotransmitter release. This results in unregulated motor activity and autonomic disturbances.
keyPoints: Only bacteria producing tetanus toxin cause disease.
Toxin undergoes retrograde transport to CNS.
Binds to presynaptic α-motor nerve terminals, affecting neurotransmission.
Cleaves VAMP2, preventing inhibitory interneuron discharge.
Results in unregulated motor activity and autonomic disturbances.

items.title: Recognizing Generalized Tetanus and Its Life-Threatening Complications
items.content: Generalized tetanus is the most common form of clinical presentation, characterized by muscle rigidity and generalized spasms. Initial symptoms include trismus (lockjaw), progressing to neck and body muscle rigidity, difficulty swallowing, and pharyngeal and laryngeal spasms. Laryngeal spasm can lead to respiratory failure or apnea, which is a life-threatening event.
keyPoints: Generalized tetanus presents with muscle rigidity and generalized spasms.
Initial symptoms include trismus (lockjaw), progressing to neck and body muscle rigidity, difficulty swallowing, pharyngeal and laryngeal spasms.
Laryngeal spasm can cause respiratory failure or apnea, a life-threatening event without immediate respiratory support.

items.title: Understanding Autonomic Nervous System Disturbance in Severe Tetanus
items.content: Autonomic nervous system disturbance (ANSD) occurs during the second week of severe tetanus. It is characterized by fluctuating heart rate and blood pressure due to sympathetic or parasympathetic alterations, leading to cardiovascular events as a major risk. Symptoms include gastrointestinal stasis, sweating, and increased tracheal secretions.
keyPoints: ANSD occurs during the second week of severe tetanus.
Characterized by fluctuating heart rate (tachycardia or bradycardia) and blood pressure (hypertension or hypotension).
Major risk is death due to cardiovascular events.
Symptoms include gastrointestinal stasis, sweating, and increased tracheal secretions.

items.title: Diagnosis of Tetanus Based on Clinical Findings
items.content: The diagnosis of tetanus is primarily clinical, as confirmatory laboratory tests are limited. Positive culture of C. tetani from wounds does not confirm tetanus. Serum antitetanus immunoglobulin G levels >0.1 IU/mL indicate protection and do not support a diagnosis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis based on clinical findings
Positive culture of C. tetani does not confirm tetanus
Serum antitetanus immunoglobulin G levels >0.1 IU/mL are protective

items.title: Differentiating Tetanus from Other Conditions
items.content: Tetanus must be differentiated from conditions such as strychnine poisoning, dystonic reactions to antidopaminergic drugs, somatic symptom disorder, stiff person syndrome, and neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Abdominal muscle rigidity in tetanus is continuous, unlike peritonitis or acute abdominal emergencies.
keyPoints: Differentiate from conditions like strychnine poisoning, dystonic reactions
Abdominal muscle rigidity in tetanus is continuous

items.title: Wound Management in Tetanus
items.content: Proper wound care, including identification, cleaning, and debridement of necrotic material, is crucial to prevent further toxin production by anaerobic bacteria. This should be done several hours after administering anti-toxin.
keyPoints: Failure to remove devitalized tissue may result in recurrent or prolonged tetanus.

items.title: Antibiotic Therapy for Tetanus
items.content: Metronidazole is preferred for antibiotic therapy in tetanus, administered at 400 mg rectally or 500 mg IV every 6 hours for 7 days. Penicillin can be used as an alternative but may exacerbate spasms and increase mortality.
keyPoints: Penicillin dose: 100,000–200,000 IU/kg per day; associated with increased mortality.

items.title: Antitoxin Administration in Tetanus
items.content: Early administration of antitoxin is essential to deactivate circulating tetanus toxin. Human tetanus immunoglobulin (HTIG) is preferred due to a lower risk of anaphylactoid reactions, with a dose range of 500–5000 IU IM.
keyPoints: HTIG dose: 500–5000 IU IM; equine antitoxin dose: 10,000–20,000 U IM after hypersensitivity testing.

items.title: Prognosis Factors in Tetanus
items.content: The prognosis of tetanus is influenced by the rapidity of symptom onset and the length of incubation period. Shorter periods from wound to first symptom and from first symptom to generalized spasm indicate a worse prognosis.
keyPoints: Shorter incubation and onset periods correlate with poorer outcomes.
Tetanus Severity Score (TSS) is superior for predicting severity, with 66% sensitivity and 91% specificity.

items.title: Prevention Strategies for Tetanus
items.content: Effective prevention of tetanus involves wound management, vaccination, and specific measures for neonatal protection. Vaccination schedules are crucial for maintaining immunity.
keyPoints: Primary vaccination coverage in infancy is 86% for DTP3.
WHO recommends a primary course starting at 6 weeks with intervals of 4 weeks; boosters follow at specified ages.
CDC suggests five doses for children and boosters every 10 years.

items.title: Impact of Maternal Vaccination on Neonatal Tetanus
items.content: Complete maternal vaccination significantly reduces neonatal tetanus incidence. WHO guidelines emphasize timely doses during pregnancy for effective prevention.
keyPoints: Maternal vaccination can reduce neonatal tetanus by 94%.
WHO recommends at least two doses before delivery, with a third dose 6 months later.

items.question: Which organism produces the neurotoxin responsible for causing tetanus?
items.explanation: Tetanus is caused by a neurotoxin produced by Clostridium tetani.

items.question: What is the most common entry site for C. tetani spores in adults?
items.explanation: The most common entry sites for C. tetani spores in adults are superficial abrasions to the limbs.

items.question: What is a significant risk factor for neonatal tetanus?
options: Home delivery with unhygienic umbilical cutting
items.explanation: Neonatal tetanus is mainly caused by home delivery with unhygienic umbilical cutting and inadequate umbilical cord care.

items.question: Which age group had the highest incidence of tetanus cases in the United States between 2009 and 2017?
options: Individuals between the ages of 20 and 64
items.explanation: Most tetanus cases occurred in individuals between the ages of 20 and 64 (64%).

items.question: What is a significant factor associated with increased risk of tetanus?
items.explanation: Diabetes and intravenous drug use were associated with increased tetanus risk.

items.question: What is the primary method of transport for tetanus toxin to reach its site of action in the nervous system?
options: Retrograde transport into the central nervous system
items.explanation: Tetanus toxin undergoes retrograde transport into the central nervous system (CNS).

items.question: Which form of tetanus is characterized by muscle rigidity and generalized spasms?
items.explanation: Generalized tetanus is the most common form of clinical presentation, characterized by muscle rigidity and generalized spasms.

items.question: What are the initial symptoms commonly seen in tetanus?
items.explanation: The most common initial symptoms of tetanus include trismus (lockjaw), which progresses to neck and body muscle rigidity.

items.question: What is the most common cause of death in tetanus if not treated promptly?
options: Respiratory failure due to laryngeal spasm
items.explanation: Laryngeal spasm can cause respiratory failure or apnea and is the most common cause of death in tetanus without immediate respiratory support.

items.question: Which laboratory test result is considered protective against a diagnosis of tetanus?
options: Positive culture of C. tetani from wounds
Serum antitetanus immunoglobulin G levels >0.1 IU/mL
Negative bioassay for serum tetanus toxin
Polymerase chain reaction detection of the tetanus neurotoxin gene
items.explanation: Levels >0.1 IU/mL of serum antitetanus immunoglobulin G are considered protective and do not support a diagnosis of tetanus.

items.question: Which condition is NOT typically included in the differential diagnosis for neonatal tetanus?
items.explanation: Somatic symptom disorder is not typically included in the differential diagnosis for neonatal tetanus.

items.question: What can be a potential confusion with cephalic tetanus?
options: Strabismus due to cranial nerve diseases
Trismus of other etiologies such as oropharyngeal infection
items.explanation: Cephalic tetanus can be confused with trismus of other etiologies such as oropharyngeal infection.

items.question: Which antibiotic is preferred for treating tetanus, and why?
options: Penicillin, because it binds to the GABA receptor.
Metronidazole, due to its effectiveness against anaerobic bacteria.
Penicillin, as it has been associated with decreased mortality in studies.
Metronidazole, because it is less likely to cause anaphylactoid reactions.
items.explanation: Metronidazole is preferred for antibiotic therapy due to its effectiveness against anaerobic bacteria.

items.question: What is the recommended dose range of human tetanus immunoglobulin (HTIG) for treating tetanus?
options: 400 mg rectally or 500 mg IV every 6 h
items.explanation: A single IM dose of HTIG ranges from 500 to 5,000 IU.

items.question: Which antitoxin is less likely to cause anaphylactoid reactions?
options: Both are equally likely to cause reactions
Neither, as both have similar reaction profiles
items.explanation: Human tetanus immunoglobulin (HTIG) is less likely to be associated with anaphylactoid reactions.

items.question: What complication can arise from treatment with diazepam injection in tetanus patients?
items.explanation: Diazepam injection can cause thrombophlebitis as a complication.

items.question: Which factor is associated with more severe disease in tetanus?
items.explanation: Rapid development of tetanus is associated with more severe disease.

items.question: What is the superior scale for predicting the severity and outcome of tetanus?
items.explanation: The Tetanus Severity Score (TSS) is superior to the Dakar and Philips scores.

items.question: What is the recommended age range for the first booster dose of tetanus vaccine according to WHO?
items.explanation: The first booster is given from 12–23 months of age.

items.question: What percentage reduction in neonatal tetanus incidence is achieved with complete maternal vaccination?
items.explanation: Complete maternal vaccination reduces the incidence of neonatal tetanus by an estimated 94%.

items.statement: The mortality rate of tetanus has increased significantly over the past two decades.
items.explanation: The mortality rate from tetanus has decreased significantly over the past two decades.

items.statement: C. tetani spores can survive in harsh environmental conditions, including boiling and many disinfectants.
items.explanation: C. tetani spores are highly resilient and resist boiling and many disinfectants.

items.statement: The global incidence of neonatal tetanus has increased since WHO's elimination program.
items.explanation: The global incidence of neonatal tetanus has reduced significantly following a concerted elimination program by WHO.

items.statement: Tetanus toxin is produced as a single 150-kDa protein that is cleaved into heavy and light chains.
items.explanation: Tetanus toxin is initially produced as a single 150-kDa protein that is cleaved to produce heavy (100-kDa) and light (50-kDa) chains.

items.statement: Autonomic nervous system disturbance (ANSD) occurs during the first week of severe tetanus.
items.explanation: ANSD occurs during the second week of severe tetanus.

items.statement: Localized tetanus can progress to generalized tetanus.
items.explanation: Localized tetanus can indeed progress to generalized tetanus.

items.statement: A positive culture of C. tetani from wounds confirms a diagnosis of tetanus.
items.explanation: Positive culture of C. tetani from wounds cannot confirm a diagnosis of tetanus.

items.statement: Polymerase chain reaction can be used to detect the tetanus neurotoxin gene.
items.explanation: Polymerase chain reaction has been developed to detect the tetanus neurotoxin gene.

items.statement: Equine-derived antitoxin is the preferred treatment for tetanus in high-income countries.
items.explanation: HTIG is preferred due to a lower risk of anaphylactoid reactions.

items.statement: Magnesium sulfate can be used to control autonomic nervous system dysfunction in tetanus.
items.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is used to manage high blood pressure and tachycardia.

items.statement: The CDC recommends five doses of tetanus vaccine for infants and children at specific ages.
items.explanation: CDC recommends five doses at specified intervals.

items.statement: As of 2022, all countries have achieved maternal and neonatal tetanus elimination.
items.explanation: 47 countries had achieved elimination by March 2022.

items.assertion: Neonatal tetanus typically presents with an inability to suck and poor feeding.
items.reason: Neonates with tetanus often have generalized spasms, which affect their ability to feed properly.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why neonatal tetanus presents with these symptoms.

items.assertion: HTIG should be administered early in tetanus treatment.
items.reason: Early administration deactivates circulating toxin and prevents uptake into the nervous system.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Patients with tetanus should be nursed in calm environments to prevent spasms.
items.reason: Light and noise can trigger spasms in patients with severe tetanus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Surviving tetanus in adults is associated with reduced long-term functional outcomes.
items.reason: Studies of children and neonates have suggested a higher incidence of neurologic sequelae.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.

items.assertion: The Tetanus Severity Score (TSS) has a sensitivity of 66% and specificity of 91%.
items.reason: It is superior to the Dakar and Philips scores for predicting tetanus severity.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why TSS is considered superior.
